{{Infobox 河川 |名称=田川 |画像=[[ファイル:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] |画像説明=幸橋から（宇都宮市） |水系等級=[[一級水系]] |水系=[[利根川]] |種別=一級河川 |延長=77.9 |標高=550 |流量=10 |観測所= |流域面積=245.8 |水源=[[日光市]]七里 |河口=[[鬼怒川]]（[[栃木県]][[小山市]]） |流域=[[栃木県]] |脚注= |出典= }} '''田川'''（たがわ）は、[[栃木県]][[日光市]]を源流とし、[[宇都宮市]]、[[下野市]]を流れ、同県[[小山市]]で'''田川放水路'''を経て[[鬼怒川]]に合流する[[一級河川]]である。旧水路は栃木県小山市から[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に至り鬼怒川に合流するが、田川放水路の整備により河川としての実質上の合流点は小山市となっている。
== 地理 == [[栃木県]][[日光市]]七里の低山地や[[志度淵川]]の分水に源を発する。日光市猪倉、[[宇都宮市]]石那田町、徳次郎町を経て宇都宮市街部を流れ、さらに宇都宮市川田町、横田、[[上三川町]]石田、[[下野市]]谷地賀、[[小山市]]延島新田から田川放水路を経て[[鬼怒川]]に[[合流]]する。田川旧水路の鬼怒川合流地点は茨城県結城市と茨城県[[筑西市]]の境となっている。
全長は78km、流域面積245.8[[平方キロメートル|km<sup>2</sup>]]は、[[鬼怒川]]支流の中で最大である。田川の支流は[[赤堀川 (栃木県)|赤堀川]]、[[山田川 (栃木県)|山田川]]など11の一級河川から成る。
田川上流部は低山地で長閑な田園地帯が広がり、[[果樹園]]や[[雑木林]]が見られる。田川中下流域は[[宇都宮市]]街地の他、[[水田]]として利用されている。
[[江戸時代]]初期には用水の取り込みが行われ、流域の水田を灌漑していたとされる一方、「暴れ川」としても知られ、氾濫に伴う流路変更があったといわれる。治水事業としては、[[用水路]]開削や[[霞堤]]などが行われてきた。
== 歴史 == 古来、田川下流は「[[暴れ川]]」であった。多雨季の氾濫によりその流路は度々変更され、氾濫域には肥沃な土壌が形成された。一方、上中流域では流れに沿って土地が削られ[[河岸段丘]]が発達し、[[谷底平野]]（[[沖積低地|沖積地]]）が形成された。
[[江戸時代]]以降、田川からは[[宇都宮藩]]や[[江戸幕府|幕府]]によって近隣地域に[[灌漑用水]]が引かれ、田畑を潤した。中でも[[二宮尊徳]]が[[宇都宮市|宇都宮]]北西部の台地に引水した[[新川 (栃木県)|宝木用水（新川）]]や、下流部[[下野市]]・[[小山市]]・[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に亘る[[吉田用水]]が著名である。
== 治水 == 田川の氾濫に対する治水事業としては、古来、[[霞堤]]の設置による堤防決壊・溢水の防止対策が取られてきた。近年では台風の被害に対する対策が取られ、逆川から鬼怒川合流部までの区間の台風対策計画は施工が完了している。今後は逆川上流部および田川支川の[[武名瀬川]]、[[御用川]]における河道改修工事等が進められることになっている。一方で宇都宮市街地の拡大など流域の都市化が進むと、降雨時の時間あたり河川流入水量が増大することから、今後は下流部での被害増大も視野に入れた浸水被害対策が必要とされている。
; [[1947年]]（昭和22年）[[9月]]の洪水 : [[カスリーン台風]]による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm越えの降雨を観測し、被害は県全域で死者300人超、浸水家屋4万戸超、田畑冠水25,000haに及んだ。 ; [[1986年]]（昭和61年）[[8月]]の洪水 : 台風10号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm程度の降雨を観測し、被害は浸水面積46平米、浸水家屋200戸超、田畑冠水で28haの被害を生じた。 ; [[1997年]]（平成9年）[[6月]]の洪水 : 台風7号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量150mm超を観測し、35haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[1998年]]（平成10年）8月の洪水 : 集中豪雨による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mmを観測した。81haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[2019年]]（令和元年）10月の洪水 : [[令和元年東日本台風]]（台風19号）による洪水。2019年10月12日15時頃から非常に強い降雨となり、明治橋水位観測所では氾濫危険水位を9時間にわたり超過した<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=栃木県県土整備部河川課県土防災対策班|title=栃木県における令和元年災害について|work=防災第857号（全国防災協会）|date=|accessdate=2022/09/19}}</ref>。同日22時30分頃には宇都宮駅西側で大規模な氾濫が発生した<ref name="bousai" />。
== 流域の自治体 == ; 栃木県 : [[日光市]]、[[宇都宮市]]、[[河内郡]][[上三川町]]、[[下野市]]、[[小山市]] ; 茨城県（旧水路のみ） : [[結城市]]
== 支流 == * 逆川（日光市） * 赤堀川 * 逆川（宇都宮市） * [[一侍川]] * 御用川 * [[釜川]] * 武名瀬川 *山田川
== 橋梁 == {{ul |栃木県日光市{{columns-list|25em| * 田川新橋（[[栃木県道70号宇都宮今市線]]） * 川窪橋 * 下田橋 * 田川橋（[[栃木県道110号下野大沢停車場線]]） * 新細内橋 * 一本橋 * 犬塚橋（[[栃木県道22号大沢宇都宮線|新里街道]]） }} |栃木県宇都宮市{{columns-list|25em| * 田川大橋（[[国道119号|日光街道]]） * 新横川橋 * 大網上橋 * 大網下橋 * 毘沙門橋 * 上横倉橋（[[国道293号]]） * 上金井大橋（[[東北自動車道]]側道） * 桜橋 * 城橋（横山街道） * 横山橋 * 瓦谷田川橋 * 田川橋（長岡街道） * 新関堀橋（[[宇都宮環状道路|宇都宮環状道路：国道119号豊郷バイパス]]） * 西所橋 * 鎌倉橋（[[栃木県道63号藤原宇都宮線|田原街道]]） * 大曽橋（[[栃木県道10号宇都宮那須烏山線|競輪場通り]]） * 錦橋 * 大錦橋（大錦橋通り） * 大泉橋（大泉橋通り） * 東橋（[[栃木県道64号宇都宮向田線|県庁前通り]]） * 幸橋（陸羽街道） * [[宮の橋]]（[[栃木県道・茨城県道1号宇都宮笠間線|大通り]]） * 押切橋（旧古多橋または小田橋） * 洗橋（[[旭町の大いちょう#いちょう通り|いちょう通り]]） * 城東橋 * 簗瀬橋（上三川街道） * 簗瀬大橋（[[栃木県道・茨城県道35号宇都宮結城線|平成通り]]） * 金井田橋 * 旭陵橋（旭陵通り） * 田川橋梁（[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[宇都宮線]]・[[日光線]]） * 新田川橋（[[国道4号]]） * 川田橋 * 平元橋（平元通り） * 横川橋 * 第二横田橋 * 田川橋梁（宇都宮環状道路：[[国道121号]]） * 中島橋 * 下反町橋 * 田町与橋 * 孫八橋（[[栃木県道193号雀宮真岡線]]） * 野ゾ花橋 * 二千年都橋（[[北関東自動車道]]側道） }} |栃木県上三川町{{columns-list|25em| * 石田橋 * 下石田橋 * 神主橋 * 川神橋 * 川中子橋（[[栃木県道71号羽生田上蒲生線]]） * 上落合橋 * 落合橋 * 明治橋（[[国道352号]]） * 蒲生橋 * 田川橋（[[新4号国道]]） * 町田橋 }} |栃木県下野市{{columns-list|25em| * 谷地賀橋（[[栃木県道310号下野二宮線]]） * 塚越橋 }} |栃木県小山市{{columns-list|25em| * 蛇続橋 * 坪山橋（[[栃木県道44号栃木二宮線]]） * 宝蔵寺橋 * 舟戸大橋（結城街道）：田川放水路 * 舟戸橋：田川放水路 * 七ツ橋：田川放水路 * 田川橋 * 天神橋 * 手こし橋 * 三宝谷橋 * 伝河原橋 * 田川橋（[[栃木県道214号福良羽川線]]） * 上梁橋 * 向田橋 * 梁橋（結城街道） * 福良橋（[[栃木県道・茨城県道204号結城二宮線]]） }} }} ''以下は旧水路上にある[[橋梁]]。'' {{ul |茨城県結城市{{columns-list|25em| * 秋谷瀬橋 * 越渡橋 * 新田川橋（[[国道50号]]） * 田川橋（[[結城バイパス]]） * 十二天橋 }} }}

56 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:田川 (利根川水系) up to rev 101251953
revision.text:
{{Infobox 河川 |名称=田川 |画像=[[ファイル:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] |画像説明=幸橋から（宇都宮市） |水系等級=[[一級水系]] |水系=[[利根川]] |種別=一級河川 |延長=77.9 |標高=550 |流量=10 |観測所= |流域面積=245.8 |水源=[[日光市]]七里 |河口=[[鬼怒川]]（[[栃木県]][[小山市]]） |流域=[[栃木県]] |脚注= |出典= }} '''田川'''（たがわ）は、[[栃木県]][[日光市]]を源流とし、[[宇都宮市]]、[[下野市]]を流れ、同県[[小山市]]で'''田川放水路'''を経て[[鬼怒川]]に合流する[[一級河川]]である。旧水路は栃木県小山市から[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に至り鬼怒川に合流するが、田川放水路の整備により河川としての実質上の合流点は小山市となっている。
== 地理 == [[栃木県]][[日光市]]七里の低山地や[[志度淵川]]の分水に源を発する。日光市猪倉、[[宇都宮市]]石那田町、徳次郎町を経て宇都宮市街部を流れ、さらに宇都宮市川田町、横田、[[上三川町]]石田、[[下野市]]谷地賀、[[小山市]]延島新田から田川放水路を経て[[鬼怒川]]に[[合流]]する。田川旧水路の鬼怒川合流地点は茨城県結城市と茨城県[[筑西市]]の境となっている。
全長は78km、流域面積245.8[[平方キロメートル|km<sup>2</sup>]]は、[[鬼怒川]]支流の中で最大である。田川の支流は[[赤堀川 (栃木県)|赤堀川]]、[[山田川 (栃木県)|山田川]]など11の一級河川から成る。
田川上流部は低山地で長閑な田園地帯が広がり、[[果樹園]]や[[雑木林]]が見られる。田川中下流域は[[宇都宮市]]街地の他、[[水田]]として利用されている。
[[江戸時代]]初期には用水の取り込みが行われ、流域の水田を灌漑していたとされる一方、「暴れ川」としても知られ、氾濫に伴う流路変更があったといわれる。治水事業としては、[[用水路]]開削や[[霞堤]]などが行われてきた。
== 歴史 == 古来、田川開世は「[[暴れ川]]」であった。多雨季の氾濫によりその流路は度々変更され、氾濫域には肥沃な土壌が形成された。一方、上中流域では流れに沿って土地が削られ[[河岸段丘]]が発達し、[[谷底平野]]（[[沖積低地|沖積地]]）が形成された。
[[江戸時代]]以降、田川からは[[宇都宮藩]]や[[江戸幕府|幕府]]によって近隣地域に[[灌漑用水]]が引かれ、田畑を潤した。中でも[[二宮尊徳]]が[[宇都宮市|宇都宮]]北西部の台地に引水した[[新川 (栃木県)|宝木用水（新川）]]や、下流部[[下野市]]・[[小山市]]・[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に亘る[[吉田用水]]が著名である。
== 治水 == 田川の氾濫に対する治水事業としては、古来、[[霞堤]]の設置による堤防決壊・溢水の防止対策が取られてきた。近年では台風の被害に対する対策が取られ、逆川から鬼怒川合流部までの区間の台風対策計画は施工が完了している。今後は逆川上流部および田川支川の[[武名瀬川]]、[[御用川]]における河道改修工事等が進められることになっている。一方で宇都宮市街地の拡大など流域の都市化が進むと、降雨時の時間あたり河川流入水量が増大することから、今後は下流部での被害増大も視野に入れた浸水被害対策が必要とされている。
; [[1947年]]（昭和22年）[[9月]]の洪水 : [[カスリーン台風]]による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm越えの降雨を観測し、被害は県全域で死者300人超、浸水家屋4万戸超、田畑冠水25,000haに及んだ。 ; [[1986年]]（昭和61年）[[8月]]の洪水 : 台風10号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm程度の降雨を観測し、被害は浸水面積46平米、浸水家屋200戸超、田畑冠水で28haの被害を生じた。 ; [[1997年]]（平成9年）[[6月]]の洪水 : 台風7号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量150mm超を観測し、35haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[1998年]]（平成10年）8月の洪水 : 集中豪雨による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mmを観測した。81haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[2019年]]（令和元年）10月の洪水 : [[令和元年東日本台風]]（台風19号）による洪水。2019年10月12日15時頃から非常に強い降雨となり、明治橋水位観測所では氾濫危険水位を9時間にわたり超過した<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=栃木県県土整備部河川課県土防災対策班|title=栃木県における令和元年災害について|work=防災第857号（全国防災協会）|date=|accessdate=2022/09/19}}</ref>。同日22時30分頃には宇都宮駅西側で大規模な氾濫が発生した<ref name="bousai" />。
== 流域の自治体 == ; 栃木県 : [[日光市]]、[[宇都宮市]]、[[河内郡]][[上三川町]]、[[下野市]]、[[小山市]] ; 茨城県（旧水路のみ） : [[結城市]]
== 支流 == * 逆川（日光市） * 赤堀川 * 逆川（宇都宮市） * [[一侍川]] * 御用川 * [[釜川]] * 武名瀬川 *山田川

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[田川 (利根川水系)]] to [[Tagawa River (Tone River System)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{Infobox 河川 |名称=田川 |画像=[[ファイル:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] |画像説明=幸橋から（宇都宮市） |水系等級=[[一級水系]] |水系=[[利根川]] |種別=一級河川 |延長=77.9 |標高=550 |流量=10 |観測所= |流域面積=245.8 |水源=[[日光市]]七里 |河口=[[鬼怒川]]（[[栃木県]][[小山市]]） |流域=[[栃木県]] |脚注= |出典= }} '''田川'''（たがわ）は、[[栃木県]][[日光市]]を源流とし、[[宇都宮市]]、[[下野市]]を流れ、同県[[小山市]]で'''田川放水路'''を経て[[鬼怒川]]に合流する[[一級河川]]である。旧水路は栃木県小山市から[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に至り鬼怒川に合流するが、田川放水路の整備により河川としての実質上の合流点は小山市となっている。
== 地理 == [[栃木県]][[日光市]]七里の低山地や[[志度淵川]]の分水に源を発する。日光市猪倉、[[宇都宮市]]石那田町、徳次郎町を経て宇都宮市街部を流れ、さらに宇都宮市川田町、横田、[[上三川町]]石田、[[下野市]]谷地賀、[[小山市]]延島新田から田川放水路を経て[[鬼怒川]]に[[合流]]する。田川旧水路の鬼怒川合流地点は茨城県結城市と茨城県[[筑西市]]の境となっている。
全長は78km、流域面積245.8[[平方キロメートル|km<sup>2</sup>]]は、[[鬼怒川]]支流の中で最大である。田川の支流は[[赤堀川 (栃木県)|赤堀川]]、[[山田川 (栃木県)|山田川]]など11の一級河川から成る。
田川上流部は低山地で長閑な田園地帯が広がり、[[果樹園]]や[[雑木林]]が見られる。田川中下流域は[[宇都宮市]]街地の他、[[水田]]として利用されている。
[[江戸時代]]初期には用水の取り込みが行われ、流域の水田を灌漑していたとされる一方、「暴れ川」としても知られ、氾濫に伴う流路変更があったといわれる。治水事業としては、[[用水路]]開削や[[霞堤]]などが行われてきた。
== 歴史 == 古来、田川開世は「[[暴れ川]]」であった。多雨季の氾濫によりその流路は度々変更され、氾濫域には肥沃な土壌が形成された。一方、上中流域では流れに沿って土地が削られ[[河岸段丘]]が発達し、[[谷底平野]]（[[沖積低地|沖積地]]）が形成された。
[[江戸時代]]以降、田川からは[[宇都宮藩]]や[[江戸幕府|幕府]]によって近隣地域に[[灌漑用水]]が引かれ、田畑を潤した。中でも[[二宮尊徳]]が[[宇都宮市|宇都宮]]北西部の台地に引水した[[新川 (栃木県)|宝木用水（新川）]]や、下流部[[下野市]]・[[小山市]]・[[茨城県]][[結城市]]に亘る[[吉田用水]]が著名である。
== 治水 == 田川の氾濫に対する治水事業としては、古来、[[霞堤]]の設置による堤防決壊・溢水の防止対策が取られてきた。近年では台風の被害に対する対策が取られ、逆川から鬼怒川合流部までの区間の台風対策計画は施工が完了している。今後は逆川上流部および田川支川の[[武名瀬川]]、[[御用川]]における河道改修工事等が進められることになっている。一方で宇都宮市街地の拡大など流域の都市化が進むと、降雨時の時間あたり河川流入水量が増大することから、今後は下流部での被害増大も視野に入れた浸水被害対策が必要とされている。
; [[1947年]]（昭和22年）[[9月]]の洪水 : [[カスリーン台風]]による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm越えの降雨を観測し、被害は県全域で死者300人超、浸水家屋4万戸超、田畑冠水25,000haに及んだ。 ; [[1986年]]（昭和61年）[[8月]]の洪水 : 台風10号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mm程度の降雨を観測し、被害は浸水面積46平米、浸水家屋200戸超、田畑冠水で28haの被害を生じた。 ; [[1997年]]（平成9年）[[6月]]の洪水 : 台風7号による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量150mm超を観測し、35haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[1998年]]（平成10年）8月の洪水 : 集中豪雨による洪水。宇都宮観測所で総雨量250mmを観測した。81haの浸水被害を生じた。 ; [[2019年]]（令和元年）10月の洪水 : [[令和元年東日本台風]]（台風19号）による洪水。2019年10月12日15時頃から非常に強い降雨となり、明治橋水位観測所では氾濫危険水位を9時間にわたり超過した<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=栃木県県土整備部河川課県土防災対策班|title=栃木県における令和元年災害について|work=防災第857号（全国防災協会）|date=|accessdate=2022/09/19}}</ref>。同日22時30分頃には宇都宮駅西側で大規模な氾濫が発生した<ref name="bousai" />。
== 流域の自治体 == ; 栃木県 : [[日光市]]、[[宇都宮市]]、[[河内郡]][[上三川町]]、[[下野市]]、[[小山市]] ; 茨城県（旧水路のみ） : [[結城市]]
== 支流 == * 逆川（日光市） * 赤堀川 * 逆川（宇都宮市） * [[一侍川]] * 御用川 * [[釜川]] * 武名瀬川 *山田川
== 橋梁 == {{ul |栃木県日光市{{columns-list|25em| * 田川新橋（[[栃木県道70号宇都宮今市線]]） * 川窪橋 * 下田橋 * 田川橋（[[栃木県道110号下野大沢停車場線]]） * 新細内橋 * 一本橋 * 犬塚橋（[[栃木県道22号大沢宇都宮線|新里街道]]） }} |栃木県宇都宮市{{columns-list|25em| * 田川大橋（[[国道119号|日光街道]]） * 新横川橋 * 大網上橋 * 大網下橋 * 毘沙門橋 * 上横倉橋（[[国道293号]]） * 上金井大橋（[[東北自動車道]]側道） * 桜橋 * 城橋（横山街道） * 横山橋 * 瓦谷田川橋 * 田川橋（長岡街道） * 新関堀橋（[[宇都宮環状道路|宇都宮環状道路：国道119号豊郷バイパス]]） * 西所橋 * 鎌倉橋（[[栃木県道63号藤原宇都宮線|田原街道]]） * 大曽橋（[[栃木県道10号宇都宮那須烏山線|競輪場通り]]） * 錦橋 * 大錦橋（大錦橋通り） * 大泉橋（大泉橋通り） * 東橋（[[栃木県道64号宇都宮向田線|県庁前通り]]） * 幸橋（陸羽街道） * [[宮の橋]]（[[栃木県道・茨城県道1号宇都宮笠間線|大通り]]） * 押切橋（旧古多橋または小田橋） * 洗橋（[[旭町の大いちょう#いちょう通り|いちょう通り]]） * 城東橋 * 簗瀬橋（上三川街道） * 簗瀬大橋（[[栃木県道・茨城県道35号宇都宮結城線|平成通り]]） * 金井田橋 * 旭陵橋（旭陵通り） * 田川橋梁（[[東日本旅客鉄道|JR]][[宇都宮線]]・[[日光線]]） * 新田川橋（[[国道4号]]） * 川田橋 * 平元橋（平元通り） * 横川橋 * 第二横田橋 * 田川橋梁（宇都宮環状道路：[[国道121号]]） * 中島橋 * 下反町橋 * 田町与橋 * 孫八橋（[[栃木県道193号雀宮真岡線]]） * 野ゾ花橋 * 二千年都橋（[[北関東自動車道]]側道） }} |栃木県上三川町{{columns-list|25em| * 石田橋 * 下石田橋 * 神主橋 * 川神橋 * 川中子橋（[[栃木県道71号羽生田上蒲生線]]） * 上落合橋 * 落合橋 * 明治橋（[[国道352号]]） * 蒲生橋 * 田川橋（[[新4号国道]]） * 町田橋 }} |栃木県下野市{{columns-list|25em| * 谷地賀橋（[[栃木県道310号下野二宮線]]） * 塚越橋 }} |栃木県小山市{{columns-list|25em| * 蛇続橋 * 坪山橋（[[栃木県道44号栃木二宮線]]） * 宝蔵寺橋 * 舟戸大橋（結城街道）：田川放水路 * 舟戸橋：田川放水路 * 七ツ橋：田川放水路 * 田川橋 * 天神橋 * 手こし橋 * 三宝谷橋 * 伝河原橋 * 田川橋（[[栃木県道214号福良羽川線]]） * 上梁橋 * 向田橋 * 梁橋（結城街道） * 福良橋（[[栃木県道・茨城県道204号結城二宮線]]） }} }} ''以下は旧水路上にある[[橋梁]]。'' {{ul |茨城県結城市{{columns-list|25em| * 秋谷瀬橋 * 越渡橋 * 新田川橋（[[国道50号]]） * 田川橋（[[結城バイパス]]） * 十二天橋 }} }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>

Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}

'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{CRD|1000300452|Details about Tagawa's water quality and history|Tochigi Prefectural Library}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}

In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}

Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = [[:en:Tone River|Tone River]] | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]] Shichiri | mouth = [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] ([[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>

'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], flows through [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], and merges with the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]], Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]] in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]] Ishida, [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]] Yachiga, and [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the [[:en:Utsunomiya Domain|Utsunomiya Domain]] and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by [[:en:Ninomiya Sontoku|Ninomiya Sontoku]] to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the [[:en:Typhoon Kathleen|Typhoon Kathleen]] caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': [[:en:Typhoon Hagibis|Typhoon Hagibis]] caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]], [[:en:Utsunomiya|Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Kaminokawa, Tochigi|Kaminokawa]], [[:en:Shimotsuke, Tochigi|Shimotsuke]], [[:en:Oyama, Tochigi|Oyama]] * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): [[:en:Yūki, Ibaraki|Yūki]]
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}

Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.

* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki}}
