[[zh:田川]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ceb:Ta-gawa]] {{Commons category|Ta River (Tochigi)}} [[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:田川]] [[ko:다가와]] {{wikidata link|Q11576724}} [[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:100 Views of Utsunomiya]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Kinu River]] [[Category:Rivers of Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Rivers of Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Tone River system]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[sn:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11576724]] Wikidata name: Tagawa River [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River}} | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}} Shichiri | mouth = {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River}} ({{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}}, Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}, flows through {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}}, and merges with the {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River}} via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}}, Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa}} Ishida, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}} Yachiga, and {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}} Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the {{ill|Utsunomiya Domain|en|Utsunomiya Domain|WD=Q11449814|lt=Utsunomiya Domain}} and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku}} to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki}}
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}

{{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River}} | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}} Shichiri | mouth = {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River}} ({{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}}, Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}, flows through {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}}, and merges with the {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River}} via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}}, Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa}} Ishida, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}} Yachiga, and {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}} Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the {{ill|Utsunomiya Domain|en|Utsunomiya Domain|WD=Q11449814|lt=Utsunomiya Domain}} and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku}} to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki}}
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Akaborigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Yamadagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{CRD|1000300452|Details about Tagawa's water quality and history|Tochigi Prefectural Library}}

During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the {{ill|Utsunomiya Domain|en|Utsunomiya Domain|WD=Q11449814|lt=Utsunomiya Domain|1=Utsunomiya Domain}} and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku|1=Ninomiya Sontoku}} to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen|1=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis|1=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya|1=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa|1=Kaminokawa, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki|1=Yūki, Ibaraki}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]]
{{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River|1=Tone River}} | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}} Shichiri | mouth = {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River|1=Kinugawa River}} ({{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}}, Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, flows through {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya|1=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}}, and merges with the {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River|1=Kinugawa River}} via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}}, Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki|1=Yūki, Ibaraki}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa|1=Kaminokawa, Tochigi}} Ishida, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}} Yachiga, and {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}} Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the {{ill|Utsunomiya Domain|en|Utsunomiya Domain|WD=Q11449814|lt=Utsunomiya Domain|1=Utsunomiya Domain}} and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku|1=Ninomiya Sontoku}} to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen|1=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis|1=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>

Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya|1=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa|1=Kaminokawa, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki|1=Yūki, Ibaraki}}
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Q11635482|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Q11470330|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[co:commons:Category:Ta River (Tochigi)]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ceb:Ta-gawa]][[ja:田川 (利根川水系)]]
{{Infobox River | name = Tagawa | image = [[File:Tagawa in 2005-06.jpg|300px]] | image_caption = View from Sai Bridge (Utsunomiya City) | river_system_class = [[First-class water system]] | river_system = {{ill|Tone River|en|Tone River|WD=Q177452|lt=Tone River|1=Tone River}} | type = First-class river | length = 77.9 km | source_elevation = 550 m | flow = 10 m³/s | observation_station = | basin_size = 245.8 km² | source = {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}} Shichiri | mouth = {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River|1=Kinugawa River}} ({{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}}, Tochigi Prefecture) | basin_countries = [[Tochigi Prefecture]] | notes = | references = }}
'''Tagawa''' (田川) is a [[first-class river]] in Japan that originates in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, flows through {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya|1=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}}, and merges with the {{ill|Kinugawa River|en|Kinugawa River|WD=Q1042040|lt=Kinugawa River|1=Kinugawa River}} via the '''Tagawa Diversion Channel''' in {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}}, Tochigi Prefecture. While the old course extended into {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki|1=Yūki, Ibaraki}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, the practical river mouth is now in Oyama due to the channel's construction.
== Geography == The Tagawa originates in the low mountains of Shichiri in Nikkō and the watershed of {{ill|Shidobuchi River|ja|志度淵川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shidobuchi River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It passes through areas like Nikkō's Inokura, Utsunomiya's Ishinata and Tokujirō towns, and Utsunomiya City Center, eventually flowing through Kawada, Yokota, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa|1=Kaminokawa, Tochigi}} Ishida, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}} Yachiga, and {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}} Enjimashinden before joining the Kinugawa River via the diversion channel.
The river's total length is 78 km, and its basin area covers 245.8 km², making it the largest tributary of the Kinugawa River. Tagawa's tributaries include 11 first-class rivers such as {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川 (栃木県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The upper reaches of Tagawa flow through serene rural landscapes, including orchards and wooded areas. The mid-to-lower reaches run through Utsunomiya's urban areas and serve as irrigation for rice paddies.
Since the early Edo period, water from the Tagawa was utilized for irrigation, helping to cultivate rice fields in the surrounding regions. However, the river was also notorious as a "raging river," frequently changing its course due to flooding. Flood control measures like canals and levees have been implemented over the years.
== History == Historically, the Tagawa was known as a "raging river," frequently altering its course due to heavy rain and flooding. Fertile soil was deposited in the floodplains, while the upper and middle reaches developed river terraces along the valley, forming alluvial plains.
During the Edo period, the river's water was diverted for irrigation by the {{ill|Utsunomiya Domain|en|Utsunomiya Domain|WD=Q11449814|lt=Utsunomiya Domain|1=Utsunomiya Domain}} and the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Notable irrigation works include {{ill|Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja|宝木用水（新川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragi Canal (Shinkawa)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, constructed by {{ill|Ninomiya Sontoku|en|Ninomiya Sontoku|WD=Q968964|lt=Ninomiya Sontoku|1=Ninomiya Sontoku}} to supply water to the northwest plateau of Utsunomiya, and the Yoshida Canal, which served downstream areas in Shimotsuke, Oyama, and Yūki.
== Flood Control == Flood control measures for the Tagawa date back to historical times, including levees to prevent overflow. In modern times, typhoon and rainfall-induced flood prevention projects have been undertaken. Construction along the river's mid-to-lower reaches, including diversion channels and channel renovations, is ongoing. Urbanization in the basin has increased the volume of rainwater entering the river during heavy rainfall, necessitating enhanced flood mitigation downstream.
Key flood events include:
* '''1947 (Shōwa 22)''': Flooding from the {{ill|Typhoon Kathleen|en|Typhoon Kathleen|WD=Q98241393|lt=Typhoon Kathleen|1=Typhoon Kathleen}} caused extensive damage, with 300+ fatalities, 40,000 flooded homes, and 25,000 ha of inundated farmland in Tochigi Prefecture. * '''1986 (Shōwa 61)''': Typhoon 10 caused significant flooding, inundating 46 ha and 200+ houses. * '''1997 (Heisei 9)''': Typhoon 7 led to 35 ha of flood damage. * '''1998 (Heisei 10)''': Heavy rain caused 81 ha of flood damage. * '''2019 (Reiwa 1)''': {{ill|Typhoon Hagibis|en|Typhoon Hagibis|WD=Q69841946|lt=Typhoon Hagibis|1=Typhoon Hagibis}} caused widespread flooding. Water levels exceeded the danger threshold for 9 hours, and significant inundation occurred in western Utsunomiya.<ref name="bousai">{{Cite web|url=https://www.zenkokubousai.or.jp/monthly/image/no857high.pdf|author=Tochigi Prefecture River Section|title=Reiwa Year 1 Disaster in Tochigi Prefecture|publisher=National Disaster Association|access-date=2022-09-19}}</ref>
== Municipalities in the Basin == * '''Tochigi Prefecture''': {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}}, {{ill|Utsunomiya|en|Utsunomiya|WD=Q200589|lt=Utsunomiya|1=Utsunomiya}}, {{ill|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|en|Kaminokawa, Tochigi|WD=Q1345604|lt=Kaminokawa|1=Kaminokawa, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|en|Shimotsuke, Tochigi|WD=Q851804|lt=Shimotsuke|1=Shimotsuke, Tochigi}}, {{ill|Oyama, Tochigi|en|Oyama, Tochigi|WD=Q374511|lt=Oyama|1=Oyama, Tochigi}} * '''Ibaraki Prefecture''' (old river course only): {{ill|Yūki, Ibaraki|en|Yūki, Ibaraki|WD=Q847569|lt=Yūki|1=Yūki, Ibaraki}}
== Tributaries == * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja|逆川 (日光市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Nikkō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Akaborigawa|ja|赤堀川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akaborigawa|1=Q11635482|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11635482}} * {{ill|Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja|逆川 (宇都宮市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakasagawa (Utsunomiya)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Ichisamuragawa|ja|一侍川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichisamuragawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Goyogawa|ja|御用川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goyogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Kamagawa|ja|釜川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamagawa|1=Kama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11648095}} * {{ill|Takesenagawa|ja|武名瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takesenagawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Yamadagawa|ja|山田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamadagawa|1=Q11470330|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11470330}}
== Bridges == {{ul |Tochigi Prefecture (Nikkō){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Shinbashi * Kawakubo Bridge * Shimoda Bridge * Tagawa Bridge * Shinhosounaibashi * Ipponbashi * Inuzukabashi }} |Tochigi Prefecture (Utsunomiya){{columns-list|25em| * Tagawa Ōhashi (Nikkō Kaidō) * Sakurabashi * Ōgane Bridge * Nishitokoro Bridge * Kamakura Bridge }} }}
